desc: LA's largest centipede — up to 6 in. Venomous — painful bite but not medically dangerous. Under rocks in desert and chaparral. Color variable.

fm.Color: Variable — tan, blue-green, orange, brown; yellow legs
fm.Venom: Painful bite; not medically significant; venomous forcipules
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal predator; fast; under rocks and bark by day
fm.Habitat: Desert, chaparral, oak woodland — under rocks

hp: Centipede — venomous predator of soil invertebrates. Under rocks/bark.

desc: Long-legged, fast centipede found indoors. Eats spiders, cockroaches, silverfish. Beneficial despite alarming appearance. Cannot seriously harm humans.

fm.Color: Tan-brown with dark stripes; very long banded legs
fm.Venom: Barely penetrates skin; harmless to humans
fm.Behavior: Extremely fast; hunts at night; beneficial indoor predator

hp: House centipede — long-legged predator of cockroaches, silverfish, and spiders in buildings. Venomous but beneficial household pest controller.

desc: Large dark millipede emerging en masse after desert rains. Slow, docile. Curls into spiral when disturbed. Exudes defensive chemicals.

fm.Color: Dark brown to black; cylindrical; many segments
fm.Defense: Curls into tight spiral; exudes benzoquinone defensive chemicals (can stain skin)
fm.Behavior: Mass emergence after rain; slow; docile; detritivore
fm.Habitat: Desert, chaparral — under rocks; emerges after rain

hp: Desert and desert-edge habitats.

desc: Introduced. Small, dark, flat millipede abundant in gardens and compost. Harmless detritivore. The millipede most people encounter.

fm.Behavior: Gregarious in moist organic matter; under pots, mulch
fm.Habitat: Gardens, greenhouses, compost — moist organic matter

hp: Greenhouse millipede — detritivore in compost and leaf litter. Introduced pantropical species. Curls when disturbed. Hydrogen cyanide defense.

desc: Large, dark, cylindrical millipede. Found in mountain woodland leaf litter. Important detritivore recycling nutrients.

fm.Color: Dark gray to brown; cylindrical; glossy
fm.Defense: Curls into ball; mild almond-scented secretion (hydrogen cyanide trace)
fm.Behavior: Slow; detritivore; rolls into ball when disturbed
fm.Habitat: Mountain woodland — oak, mixed forest leaf litter

hp: Detritivore in leaf litter — nutrient cycling. Preys on ant. Montane.

desc: Bristle millipede — tiny (3mm) millipede covered in tufted bristles, looking more like a fuzzy larva than a millipede. Under bark and stones. When threatened, detaches tufts of hooked bristles that entangle ant mandibles — a unique defense. One of the most primitive millipede lineages.

fm.Color: Gray-brown; covered in distinctive bristle tufts
fm.Defense: Bristles detach and entangle predators (like velcro)
fm.Behavior: Under loose bark in small groups; easily overlooked
fm.Habitat: Under bark, in crevices — oaks, pines

hp: Builds irregular tangle web. On tree bark.

desc: Green centipede — medium centipede (50-70mm), smaller than the common S. polymorpha. Greenish with dark crossbands. Mountain and foothill habitats in oak woodland. 21 leg pairs. Venomous bite — painful but not medically significant. Under rocks and bark.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; under rocks; aggressive if disturbed

hp: On oak bark. Part of bark-dwelling arthropod community. On Quercus (oak). Montane (50-70mm).

desc: Not a true centipede — a symphylan. Tiny, white, 12-legged soil dweller. Root pest of crops. Extremely common but rarely noticed.

fm.Color: White, translucent; 12 pairs of legs; antennae
fm.Behavior: Soil-dwelling; damages plant roots; very fast in soil
fm.Habitat: Agricultural and garden soil — root zone

hp: Not a centipede — symphylan. Root pest of crops. In soil — feeds on root hairs and organic matter.

desc: Slender blind centipede — reddish-brown scolopendromorph of oak woodland leaf litter. Smaller (40-50mm) and more slender than Scolopendra. Lacks eyes. 23 leg pairs. Less venomous than larger centipedes. Under deeply embedded rocks and rotting logs in moist habitats.
hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). Associated with Coast Live Oak. On Quercus (oak). Woodland (40-50mm).

desc: Hook-clawed millipede — large dark cylindrical millipede (60-80mm) common in SoCal oak woodland leaf litter. Curls into tight defensive spiral when disturbed, secreting benzoquinone compounds. Important decomposer — shreds leaf litter, accelerating nutrient cycling. Native.

fm.Defense: Curls into tight spiral; secretes defensive chemicals

hp: Oak; decomposer. Associated with Coast Live Oak.

desc: Stone centipede — brown 15-legged lithobiomorph (30mm) under rocks and logs. European introduction, now cosmopolitan. Fast predator of small invertebrates. Named for forceps-like last leg pair. Very common in LA gardens. Molts throughout life, adding leg pairs with each molt.
hp: Predatory — hunts arthropod prey. Beneficial in gardens.

desc: Pitted soil centipede — eyeless, elongated geophilid centipede (40-60mm) living entirely in soil. 39-49 leg pairs — the high count is diagnostic of Geophilomorpha. Predator of soil invertebrates including earthworms. Named for pitted ventral pores that secrete luminescent fluid.
hp: Soil predator — hunts in leaf litter.

desc: Flat-backed millipede — flat brownish millipede with prominent lateral keels on each segment. Detritivore in leaf litter, gardens, and compost. European introduction. Produces hydrogen cyanide when disturbed — smells of almonds. 20 body segments, each with lateral paranota.

fm.Defense: Produces hydrogen cyanide — smells of almonds

hp: Detritivore in leaf litter — nutrient cycling. On almond.

desc: Saussure's millipede — dark millipede with contrasting pale/yellow legs. Garden and greenhouse species. Introduced from tropical Asia. Detritivore in compost and mulch. Part of the Paradoxosomatidae — the largest family of flat-backed millipedes. Coils when disturbed.
hp: Garden millipede — detritivore in compost and mulch. Introduced from tropical Asia. Coils defensively. Produces hydrogen cyanide.

desc: Small dark millipede endemic to Santa Catalina Island. One of very few invertebrates restricted to the Channel Islands.

fm.Habitat: Catalina Island — leaf litter, under rocks

hp: Endemic to Santa Catalina Island. Detritivore in island leaf litter. Conservation-relevant endemic.

desc: North America's largest centipede — up to 8 in. Distinctive red head and black body with yellow legs. Painful venomous bite.

fm.Color: Red head; dark body; yellow legs

hp: Largest NA centipede — to 200mm (Shelley 2002). Predator of large insects, small vertebrates. Painful venomous bite.

desc: Dark millipede with yellow side spots. Contains cyanide — smells like almonds when handled. Common in coastal forests. Native Pacific Coast species.

fm.Warning: Contains cyanide — almond smell when handled

hp: Millipede — detritivore shredding leaf litter. Defensive chemicals deter predators.

desc: Medium-large centipede with brown-orange coloring. Native to California coastal areas. Nocturnal predator. Can deliver painful bite.
hp: Citrus associate — on orange, lemon, grapefruit (Rutaceae).

desc: Rear-born centipede — small-medium (30-40mm) dark reddish centipede under bark, especially eucalyptus. Flattened body adapted for bark crevices. 15 leg pairs. Named 'posticus' (rear) for posterior birth of young. Very common under eucalyptus bark throughout LA. Native.
hp: On eucalyptus — introduced with host tree. On Eucalyptus (eucalyptus). Flattened body (30-40mm).

desc: Claremont millipede — large dark millipede of coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Up to 4 inches. Curls into tight spiral when disturbed. Cyanide-producing defensive secretion.

fm.Warning: Cyanide secretion — wash hands after handling

hp: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Associated with Black Sage. Size: 4 in.

desc: Claremont flat-backed millipede — small flat-backed millipede with lateral keels. Native to SoCal chaparral. Under bark and rocks. Important leaf litter decomposer.
hp: Detritivore in leaf litter — nutrient cycling. Preys on ant. Chaparral.

desc: Garden cryptops — medium soil centipede. Eyeless. 21 pairs of legs. Lives in soil and under debris. Predatory on small soil invertebrates. Common in gardens.
hp: Soil predator — hunts in leaf litter. Beneficial in gardens. Family Cryptopidae.

desc: Novara flat millipede — introduced flat-backed millipede from tropics. Found in gardens and greenhouses. Dark with pale lateral keels. Leaf litter decomposer.
hp: Detritivore in leaf litter — nutrient cycling.

desc: Reigning soil centipede — elongated pale centipede with 40+ leg pairs living in soil. Predatory on earthworms and soil invertebrates. Common in garden soil.
hp: Soil predator — hunts in leaf litter. Beneficial in gardens. Elongated.

desc: Cryptopid centipede — small eyeless soil centipede. 21 pairs of legs. Predatory on small soil invertebrates. Under rocks and in leaf litter.
hp: Soil predator — hunts in leaf litter. Family Cryptopidae.

desc: Spotted snake millipede — small pale elongated millipede with row of red spots along sides. Introduced from Europe. Pest of root crops — attacks potato tubers. Common in gardens.
hp: Garden and crop pest. Herbivore on cultivated plants. On potato. Elongated.

desc: Confluent centipede — medium soil centipede (geophilid). Long thin body with many legs. In soil and under rocks. Predatory on small soil organisms.
hp: Soil predator — hunts in leaf litter. Family Geophilidae.

desc: Monica millipede — BIOLUMINESCENT millipede that glows green-blue in darkness. One of the few bioluminescent millipedes in the world. Mountain chaparral. Remarkable.

fm.Color: Brown by day; GLOWS GREEN-BLUE at night
fm.Note: Bioluminescent — one of few glowing millipedes in world

hp: Native chaparral scrub habitat. Bioluminescent — produces light.

desc: Common soil centipede — slender elongated geophilid centipede in soil and under rocks. Many leg pairs (50+). Predatory on small soil invertebrates.

fm.Color: Pale yellow-brown; very thin; many legs

hp: Soil predator — hunts in leaf litter. Elongated.

desc: Furry snake millipede — introduced European millipede. Cylindrical. Fine hairs on body. Under logs and in leaf litter. Common in moist gardens.
hp: Snake millipede — elongated European millipede in garden leaf litter. Detritivore shredding fallen leaves. Important in soil nutrient cycling.

desc: Few-toothed soil centipede — small geophilid centipede. In soil. Predatory. Part of the diverse but hidden soil centipede community.
hp: Soil predator — hunts in leaf litter. Family Geophilidae. Small geophilid centipede. In soil. Part of the d.

desc: Fused soil centipede — slender geophilid centipede in soil and under debris. Many leg pairs. Predatory on minute soil fauna.
hp: Soil predator — hunts in leaf litter. Family Geophilidae.

desc: Glendora soil centipede — geophilid centipede. Named for Glendora, CA. In soil of foothill habitats. Part of the local endemic centipede fauna.
hp: Soil predator — geophilid centipede hunting in foothill soils. Named for Glendora, CA.

desc: San Clemente flat-backed millipede — introduced flat-backed millipede. On San Clemente Island and mainland. Under debris and bark. Important decomposer.
hp: Detritivore — breaks down organic matter. Preys on ant. Flattened body.

desc: Beautiful millipede — medium cylindrical millipede named for Hilton. In leaf litter and under logs. Important decomposer of plant material.
hp: Detritivore in leaf litter — nutrient cycling. Preys on ant. Family Spirobolidae.

desc: Monica soil centipede — geophilid centipede named for Santa Monica area. In soil of foothill habitats. Predatory.
hp: Soil predator — hunts in leaf litter. Foothill habitat.

desc: Claremont soil centipede — geophilid centipede named for Claremont, CA. In soil. Predatory. Many leg pairs. Part of local endemic centipede fauna.
hp: Soil predator — hunts in leaf litter.
